text: When a malicious file hash is identified, review where the file was observed, whether it executed, and which hosts or users were exposed.
For a suspicious executable, document file metadata, submission history, detection consistency, and any related endpoint activity.
When a file shows strong malicious detections, correlate it with process creation, persistence locations, and outbound network traffic.
For a suspicious IP address, determine whether communication was inbound or outbound, which systems were involved, and whether the timing matches other alerts.
When investigating an external IP, correlate firewall, proxy, DNS, and endpoint telemetry to understand usage context.
For an IP with poor reputation, verify whether it belongs to common cloud infrastructure before drawing conclusions.
When a suspicious domain is identified, review registration context, usage patterns, related URLs, and whether it appears in email or browser activity.
For domains referenced in multiple alerts, identify affected users, systems, and possible delivery mechanisms such as email or web access.
When analyzing a suspicious URL, review redirection behavior, hosting context, access source, and any resulting download or execution activity.
For URLs linked to user activity, correlate access timestamps with browser, proxy, and endpoint telemetry to assess possible compromise.
When suspicious email indicators are present, review sender identity, authentication signals, embedded links, attachment metadata, and delivery scope.
For email-related investigations, document user interaction, message spread, and any follow-on endpoint or network events.
When triaging a CVE, evaluate affected asset exposure, relevant software version, exploit maturity, and any telemetry that suggests exploitation.
For vulnerabilities on exposed services, correlate asset inventory data with process, authentication, and network evidence.
When multiple indicators appear together, build a timeline showing submission, access, execution, and network activity to improve interpretation.
For suspicious artifacts with limited evidence, assess confidence by comparing reputation, detections, prevalence, and supporting telemetry.
When repeated alerts reference the same indicator, determine whether they represent a single event, recurring activity, or a broader campaign.
For new or low-prevalence indicators, prioritize contextual analysis such as affected host role, user activity, and timing relative to other alerts.
When threat intelligence references are present, compare them with local telemetry to decide whether the indicator is relevant to the environment.
For ambiguous indicators, document both malicious and benign explanations and weigh them against available evidence.
When a file hash shows zero detections and a clean verdict, document it as benign, close the investigation, and whitelist if it appears in recurring alerts.
When an IP shows zero malicious detections and low abuse score, mark it as clean, document the result and whitelist if it generates repeated false alerts.
When a domain has no malicious detections and a clean reputation, close the investigation, document as benign and add to whitelist if needed.
When a URL is clean across all vendors with a low risk score, mark the alert as false positive, document the result and whitelist the URL if appropriate.
When an email address passes all authentication checks and has no blacklist alerts, close the investigation, classify as legitimate and document the result.
